Open source notebook

Assignment / Image Classification

PlantVillage Image Classification Report

Interactive report extracted from Image Classfication.ipynb. This page keeps the same polished pipeline-report style as your HTML mẫu, but rewrites it for the PlantVillage image classification workflow: dataset audit, preprocessing, augmentation, model strategies, final comparison, and Swin-based error analysis.

Notebook Assets

Open notebook
Dataset: PlantVillage (TFDS) Framework: PyTorch + timm Models: ResNet50 / Swin-Tiny / transfer learning Completed runs: 3 saved strategies

Conclusion

The notebook shows a complete and reliable image-classification workflow: dataset auditing, stratified 70 / 15 / 15 splitting, ImageNet normalization, augmentation, weighted sampling, and transfer learning on pretrained backbones. Across the three completed runs, the main pattern is clear: partial fine-tuning is the right strategy, while freezing most of the backbone causes a large performance drop.

Even though resnet50_partial gives the strongest held-out test score (Test Accuracy = 0.990566, Test Macro F1 = 0.988270), the final model selected for this report is swint_tiny_partial.

The reason is practical rather than cosmetic: Swin-Tiny achieves the best validation performance (best_val_macro_f1 = 0.989041, val_accuracy = 0.989564), keeps training time comparable to ResNet50, uses fewer trainable parameters than resnet50_partial, and is also the checkpoint used in the notebook's phase-2 error analysis and saliency inspection. That makes it the most coherent model to carry forward as the chosen project model.

The notebook first wraps the TensorFlow Datasets source into a PyTorch-friendly dataset that can return both tensors for training and metadata dictionaries for EDA. That makes the same raw source reusable across auditing, splitting, visualization, and training.

Exploratory Data Analysis From The Notebook

Random Sample Grid

The notebook starts by drawing random examples to verify that classes really differ in visual texture, lesion shape, and color distribution before modeling.

Data Quality Audit

The anomaly scan checks every example for corruption, unsupported structures, and zero-sized images. The saved output reports a completely clean dataset.

Corrupt files: 0 Unsupported format: 0 Zero-sized images: 0 Total images checked: 54,303

This matters because it means training errors later are much more likely to come from class overlap or model behavior than from broken input files.

Metadata Summary

The metadata scan shows a highly standardized corpus: images are decoded as RGB, every class stays at 256 × 256, and aspect ratio remains 1.0 throughout the dataset.

Main format: decoded_from_tfds Color mode: RGB Width range: 256 → 256 Height range: 256 → 256

Train / Validation / Test Split

The notebook applies a per-class stratified split, so each class preserves the same approximate ratio across the three subsets.

SplitSamplesShare
Train37,99670.0%
Validation8,14515.0%
Test8,16215.0%

The split is structurally clean, but not balanced. The training set ranges from only 106 images for Potato___healthy to 3,854 images for Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening), producing an imbalance ratio of 36.36×.

Pipeline Selector

Quick Navigation

Dataset Source Anomaly Scan Split Strategy Augmentation & Loader EDA Summary
Imbalance Handling Model Initialization Training Engine Strategy Comparison Error Analysis

Data & Preprocessing Pipeline

Dataset Source And PyTorch Wrapper

The notebook downloads plant_village from TensorFlow Datasets, stores it in ArrayRecord format for random access, then wraps it into a custom PyTorch dataset. 

builder = tfds.builder(
    "plant_village",
    data_dir=DATA_DIR,
    file_format="array_record",
)
builder.download_and_prepare()

plant_source = builder.as_data_source(split="train")
label_names = builder.info.features["label"].names

This is a strong design choice: the same raw dataset can expose PIL images and metadata for EDA, while still serving normalized tensors for training later.

Anomaly Scan

Before any split or transform is applied, the pipeline verifies that each record is structurally valid. 

for idx in tqdm(range(len(eda_dataset)), desc="Checking anomalies"):
    sample = eda_dataset[idx]
    img = sample["image"]

    if not isinstance(img, Image.Image):
        anomalies["unsupported_format"].append(...)
    elif img.size[0] == 0 or img.size[1] == 0:
        anomalies["size_zero"].append(...)
    else:
        arr = np.array(img)

The final report shows zero corrupt files, zero unsupported images, and zero zero-sized examples, so no manual cleaning step is needed before training.

Stratified 70 / 15 / 15 Split

The split happens class by class, not globally, which protects rare labels from being underrepresented in validation or test. 

for label in labels:
    label_df = records_df[records_df["label_name"] == label]
    indices = label_df["index"].tolist()

    train_idx, temp_idx = train_test_split(indices, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)
    val_idx, test_idx = train_test_split(temp_idx, test_size=0.5, random_state=42)

    train_indices.extend(train_idx)
    val_indices.extend(val_idx)
    test_indices.extend(test_idx)

Because this is done independently for each label, even the smallest classes still appear in all three subsets. That makes macro F1 a trustworthy metric later.

Augmentation, Normalization, And DataLoaders

The training branch uses stronger augmentation with crop, flip, rotation, and color jitter, while validation and test use a deterministic resize pipeline. The loader configuration also keeps BATCH_SIZE = 32, IMG_SIZE = 224, and USE_WEIGHTED_SAMPLER = True.

Batch size: 32 Image size: 224 × 224 Num workers: 2 if CUDA else 0 Weighted sampler: enabled 
BATCH_SIZE = 32
NUM_WORKERS = 2 if torch.cuda.is_available() else 0
IMG_SIZE = 224
USE_WEIGHTED_SAMPLER = True

IMAGENET_MEAN = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]
IMAGENET_STD  = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]

train_transform = T.Compose([
    T.RandomResizedCrop(
        size=IMG_SIZE,
        scale=(0.8, 1.0),
        ratio=(0.75, 1.33),
        interpolation=T.InterpolationMode.BILINEAR
    ),
    T.RandomHorizontalFlip(p=0.5),
    T.RandomRotation(degrees=15),
    T.ColorJitter(
        brightness=0.2,
        contrast=0.2,
        saturation=0.2,
        hue=0.05
    ),
    T.ToTensor(),
    T.Normalize(mean=IMAGENET_MEAN, std=IMAGENET_STD)
])

eval_transform = T.Compose([
    T.Resize((IMG_SIZE, IMG_SIZE), interpolation=T.InterpolationMode.BILINEAR),
    T.ToTensor(),
    T.Normalize(mean=IMAGENET_MEAN, std=IMAGENET_STD)
])

This setup is well matched to transfer learning: normalization follows ImageNet statistics for pretrained backbones, the training transform improves robustness to viewpoint and color variation, and the evaluation transform stays fixed so validation and test remain comparable.

Keeping USE_WEIGHTED_SAMPLER = True is especially useful here because the class distribution is highly imbalanced, so minority classes are sampled more consistently during training.

Main Takeaways From The EDA Branch

  • The raw dataset is clean: no corruption, no malformed dimensions, and no empty images were detected.
  • All samples are already standardized to square RGB images, which simplifies downstream preprocessing.
  • The class distribution is strongly imbalanced, so class-aware training choices are necessary.
  • The notebook stores enough metadata to support later error analysis by filename and class name.

Modeling, Results & Error Analysis

Imbalance Handling

Class counts are converted into inverse-frequency weights, then passed to CrossEntropyLoss. The resulting weight range is 0.2594 → 9.4330.

Smallest train class: Potato___healthy (106) Largest train class: Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening) (3,854) Weight ratio: 36.3585×

This setup helps prevent the network from simply optimizing for dominant classes such as Soybean___healthy or Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.

Model Initialization

Three pretrained backbones are defined in the notebook: ResNet50, ViT-Base, and Swin-Tiny. The saved run later trains two ResNet strategies and one Swin strategy. 

class ResNetPlantVillageClassifier(PlantVillageClassifier):
    def __init__(self, num_classes, freeze_backbone=False, dropout_rate=0.3,
                 unfreeze_backbone_layers=None):
        self.backbone = timm.create_model("resnet50", pretrained=True, num_classes=num_classes)
        ...
        if freeze_backbone:
            self.freeze_backbone_layers()
            if unfreeze_backbone_layers is not None:
                self.unfreeze_backbone_layers(unfreeze_backbone_layers)
resnet_strategy_configs = {
    "resnet50_freeze": {
        "freeze_backbone": True,
        "unfreeze_backbone_layers": None
    },
    "resnet50_partial": {
        "freeze_backbone": True,
        "unfreeze_backbone_layers": 2
    }
}

swint_tiny_partial_model = SwinTPlantVillageClassifier(
    num_classes=num_classes,
    freeze_backbone=True,
    unfreeze_backbone_layers=1
)

The core design idea is consistent across backbones: freeze most pretrained layers, then selectively unfreeze only the final high-level blocks or stages.

Training Engine

Every experiment uses the same training wrapper with early stopping, gradient clipping, checkpointing by best validation macro F1, and final evaluation on both validation and test. 

result = run_experiment(
    model_name=model_name,
    model=model,
    train_loader=train_loader,
    val_loader=val_loader,
    test_loader=test_loader,
    criterion=criterion,
    device=device,
    class_names=label_names,
    max_epochs=6,
    patience=3,
    grad_clip=1.0,
)

Because all finished strategies pass through the same training engine, the final comparison is fair: differences come from the model strategy, not from evaluation drift.

Completed Strategy Comparison

The final report keeps the three completed runs you provided: one Swin-Tiny partial fine-tune run and two ResNet50 strategies. The table below preserves the original experiment fields (best_val_macro_f1, val_accuracy, val_macro_f1, test_accuracy, test_macro_f1, runtime, inference speed, and parameter counts).

ModelBest epochBest Val Macro F1Val AccVal Macro F1Test AccTest Macro F1Train time (min)Test ms / imageTotal paramsTrainable params
resnet50_partial60.9846730.9884590.9846730.9905660.98827039.9372503.64273223,585,89422,140,966
swint_tiny_partial60.9890410.9895640.9890410.9879930.98531039.5756733.79820027,548,57615,395,798
resnet50_freeze60.8426880.8666670.8426880.8616760.83658438.6061623.72341723,585,89477,862

The comparison can be read in two layers. On pure held-out test metrics, resnet50_partial is the strongest run. However, the gap to swint_tiny_partial is small, and both models are clearly far above resnet50_freeze.

For model selection, this report prioritizes the more stable validation signal, efficient fine-tuning, and alignment with the notebook's interpretability branch. Under that view, swint_tiny_partial becomes the better project choice: it has the best validation macro F1, similar runtime, fewer trainable parameters than ResNet50 partial, and it is the exact checkpoint examined in the later error-analysis pipeline.

In other words, ResNet50 partial is the best single benchmark result, but Swin-Tiny partial is the model selected for the final system.

Final Ranking Overview

Error Analysis Branch

Phase 2 of the notebook reloads the Swin-Tiny partial checkpoint, runs it again on the test split, and stores per-sample predictions, confidences, and probabilities for deeper inspection rather than only reporting aggregate metrics. 

pred_df, test_probs = collect_predictions_dataframe(
    model=phase2_swint_model,
    loader=test_loader,
    device=device,
    class_names=label_names
)

mis_df = pred_df[pred_df["correct"] == False].sort_values("confidence", ascending=False)
confident_wrong_df = mis_df[mis_df["confidence"] >= 0.80]
Checkpoint analyzed: Swin-Tiny partial Misclassified samples: 99 Confident wrong (≥ 0.80): 57 Hard correct retained: 50

This part matters because it explains why a very accurate model still fails on some images. The errors are not random. Most of them come from disease pairs with highly similar visual symptoms: small spots, mottled texture, yellowing patterns, or lesion layouts that overlap strongly across classes, especially inside the same crop family such as tomato and corn.

The presence of 57 confident wrong predictions shows that several mistakes are genuine fine-grained confusions, not just low-confidence guesses. In practice, this usually means the model has learned meaningful disease features, but two classes still share nearly identical local evidence at the image level. Differences in lighting, leaf orientation, growth stage, background clutter, and the severity of infection can make those borderline cases even harder.

This is also one reason Swin-Tiny is a sensible final choice for the report: the notebook already uses it for the full interpretability branch, so the selected model is not only accurate but also easier to discuss with concrete confusion pairs, hard examples, and saliency maps.

The strongest confusion is between Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus and Tomato___Bacterial_spot with 18 errors.

Most Confident Wrong Predictions

These samples are especially important because they show the model is not simply uncertain; it is confidently choosing the wrong disease among visually close alternatives. That makes them the best candidates for targeted data collection, class-specific augmentation, and later confidence calibration.

Final Report Summary

The overall pipeline is strong from end to end: the dataset audit is clean, preprocessing is consistent, augmentation is appropriate for transfer learning, and the completed experiments confirm that partial fine-tuning is clearly better than freezing the backbone.

For the final project choice, this report selects Swin-Tiny (Partial Fine-tune). It is not the highest test-score run by a large margin, but it gives the best validation macro F1, remains highly competitive on the test set, uses a more efficient fine-tuning profile than ResNet50 partial, and matches the notebook's full error-analysis and saliency branch. That makes the story of the report cleaner and the chosen model easier to justify from both performance and interpretability.

The next direction is straightforward: add more hard examples for the most confused tomato and corn disease pairs, strengthen augmentation specifically for subtle lesion patterns, and test calibration or class-aware loss variants to reduce the number of high-confidence mistakes. With those improvements, Swin-Tiny is a strong candidate to move forward as the deployed model in the final system.

Source references inside this page come from the uploaded notebook outputs and the HTML pipeline style you provided.
